coredata.dc:title: Ultra-high solar steam generation based on regulated water management strategy in 3D biomass hydrogels inspired by the “binding effect” of cell walls

coredata.dc:description: Facing the global challenge of water scarcity, solar-driven seawater desalination has been emerged as a green, non-polluting and sustainable technology, but the practical application of existing solar evaporators was hindered by their low evaporation efficiency and poor mechanical property. Inspired by the binding effect of cell walls, a novel and high-efficiency three-dimensional (3D) evaporator (CDs/CF/CS-LF) was constructed, in which loofah (LF) served as a natural 3D skeleton to support the hydrogel evaporator, resisted deformation, and created an efficient dual water supply mode with the chitosan (CS) hydrogel ensuring that the evaporator maintains high evaporation rate even under high salt environment. Additionally, the cellulose nanofibers (CF) filler enhanced the mechanical property of the hydrogel, ensuring shape stability under pressure impact, and carbon dots (CDs) facilitated the aggregation and cross-linking of chitosan chains through hydrogen bonds, further improving the mechanical property of the evaporator, while also enhancing the photothermal conversion capability of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator as a photothermal material. The experimental results indicated that the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator could achieve an ultra-high solar vapor production rate of 8.08 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sunlight intensity in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Outdoor seawater desalination experiments demonstrated the utility of 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator for practical applications. This work provided a new approach for the improvement of CS-based evaporators and demonstrated their potential application in seawater desalination and purification.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facing the global challenge of water scarcity, solar-driven seawater desalination has been emerged as a green, non-polluting and sustainable technology, but the practical application of existing solar evaporators was hindered by their low evaporation efficiency and poor mechanical property. Inspired by the binding effect of cell walls, a novel and high-efficiency three-dimensional (3D) evaporator (CDs/CF/CS-LF) was constructed, in which loofah (LF) served as a natural 3D skeleton to support the hydrogel evaporator, resisted deformation, and created an efficient dual water supply mode with the chitosan (CS) hydrogel ensuring that the evaporator maintains high evaporation rate even under high salt environment. Additionally, the cellulose nanofibers (CF) filler enhanced the mechanical property of the hydrogel, ensuring shape stability under pressure impact, and carbon dots (CDs) facilitated the aggregation and cross-linking of chitosan chains through hydrogen bonds, further improving the mechanical property of the evaporator, while also enhancing the photothermal conversion capability of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator as a photothermal material. The experimental results indicated that the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator could achieve an ultra-high solar vapor production rate of 8.08 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sunlight intensity in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Outdoor seawater desalination experiments demonstrated the utility of 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator for practical applications. This work provided a new approach for the improvement of CS-based evaporators and demonstrated their potential application in seawater desalination and purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Although the Earth is rich in water resources, the proportion available for direct consumption and industrial use is extremely limited, including only part of rivers, lakes and groundwater, accounting for only 0.26 % of the planet's total water volume [1–3]. In the face of global water scarcity and the burgeoning demand for freshwater, solar-driven seawater desalination has been widely recognized as a critical solution owing to its sustainability and renewability [4,5]. While, challenges such as thermal losses at the photothermal interface and salt clogging during the solar-driven evaporation process significantly reduced the lifespan of solar evaporators, necessitating urgent solutions to ensure the long-term, stable, and efficient operation [6].

Recently, numerous efforts have demonstrated the outstanding potential advantage of hydrogel-based evaporators with a three-dimensional (3D) porous hydrophilic network that provides a continuous water supply to the heated area, while its microchannels facilitated the reflection and scattering of internal light, and captured more light energy, which was attractive for solar steam generation [7–9]. Nevertheless, most conventional hydrogel-based evaporators are usually chemically synthetic hydrogels formed by chemical crosslinking that, despite maintaining structural integrity under prolonged immersion, are environmentally friendly and may release containing toxic chemical crosslinking agents that in turn contaminate the collected fresh water [10–12]. In contrast, physical hydrogels based on natural polymers, such as chitosan, sodium alginate, nano cellulose, etc. [13–15], exhibited admirable advantages, including low toxicity, superb biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness, along with excellent hydrophilicity and water absorption capability. Although a chemical cross-linking agent is not required for the synthesis of a physical hydrogels, they have poor mechanical properties, are tended to disintegrate under prolonged immersion, losing their evaporative function, and are prone to breakage under sudden loads in practical applications [16]. Consequently, it remains challenging to enhance and modify physical hydrogel-based evaporators for ensuring their efficient and long-term stable operation during the seawater desalination.

In order to significantly overcome the deficiencies in the mechanical properties of physical hydrogels, the following three subtle strategies were commonly employed: (i) selection and incorporation of specific fillers and distributing them uniformly in the hydrogel could efficiently disperse and transfer the stress [17–19]; (ii) introduction of a strong skeleton structure, which provides strong physical support for the hydrogel, can not only effectively limit the sharp shape deformation of the hydrogel when the external conditions change, but also improve the overall strength and stability of the material [20]; (iii) incorporation of materials with rich in oxygen-containing functional groups to promote cross-linking of hydrogels, further boosting the structural durability of the hydrogels [21–23]. Based on above analysis, how to integrate multiple modification strategies together may be the best solution to the physical hydrogel-based evaporators.

Inspired by the “binding effect” of cell walls, a 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF composite hydrogel solar evaporator was successfully developed by synergistically reinforcing the chitosan (CS)-based hydrogel with cellulose nanofibers (CF), loofah fiber (LF) and carbon dots (CDs), which realized high-efficient water supply, enhanced the mechanical property, and also improved the photothermal conversion capability. Among the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF composite hydrogel solar evaporator, CF as an emerging nanomaterial, could significantly improve the mechanical properties of hydrogels by constructing a solid network structure in the matrix through its abundant hydrogen bonding and large surface area. More significantly, inspired by the cell wall-“binding effect”, an innovative introduction of the LF as the supporting skeleton effectively suppressed the shape deformation caused by changes in the external environment, while the pore structure of the LF skeleton facilitated the rapid water transport. In addition, combining CDs as a photothermal material and utilizing their high absorption and biocompatibility [24,25], not only promoted the cross-linking of the hydrogel through the oxygen-containing functional groups of the CDs, but also enhanced the photothermal performance of the CS-based hydrogel evaporator. The experimental results revealed as-prepared 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator exhibited significant photothermal conversion efficiency (93.75 %), ultra-high evaporation rate (8.08 kg m−2 h−1), and cost-effectiveness (236.6 g/(h*$)), which was superior to most CS-based evaporators in recent reports. Furthermore, in an outdoor desalination experiment, the desalination unit produced about 34.88 kg m−2 per day, demonstrating the utility of the evaporator in practical applications. This research presents a bionic design concept to regulated water management for constructing a high-efficient biomass evaporator, demonstrating the potential applications in seawater desalinization.

Chitosan (CS), acetic acid, nanocellulose fiber (CF), sodium chloride (NaCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. All chemicals were used directly and without further purification. Carbon dots (CDs) solid powders were prepared in the laboratory using an electrochemical method based on previous literature [26], which can be confirmed by using the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Fig.S1. Loofah (LF) and expanded polystyrene (EPE) was purchased from Taobao online website.

Firstly, CDs (0.15 g), chitosan powder (0.5 g) were dispersed in 0.2 M (mol/L) glacial acetic acid (25 mL) and magnetically stirred at 50 °C for 4 h to form a homogeneous black chitosan solution (0.6 wt%). Then, different concentrations of CF solution were added dropwise to the prepared CDs-CS solution and stirred magnetically at 50 °C for 2 h to obtain CDs/CF/CS solution. Finally, the obtained CDs/CF/CS solution was poured into a beaker in which LF was placed, and the solution was allowed to solidify by overnight storage in a refrigerator at −20 °C. Afterwards, the solidified samples were lyophilized in a freeze-dryer at −45 °C until all solvents were completely removed, leaving CDs/CF/CS-LF aerogels. To obtain hydrogels, the as-prepared aerogels were soaked in 0.5 M NaOH solution for 2 h, then rinsed several times with deionized water and dried normally. For the comparison, CS, CS-LF, CF/CS-LF, CDs/CS-LF hydrogels were fabricated following the same procedures without adding the distinguished components.

Solar evaporation experiments were measured on CS, CS-LF, CF/CS-LF, CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogels under room temperature of ~20 °C and relative humidity of ~42 % with a solar simulator (PLS-SXE300+/300UV, Beijing Perfect Light Company), and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution was applied as simulated seawater. For each experiment, a solar power meter was applied to test the solar flux. For the experiments, the sunlight was applied vertically to the upper surface of the samples. The water loss mass was recorded using an electronic precision balance (accuracy of 0.1 mg). And the dark evaporation flux was tested under dark conditions. In order to explore the evaporation rate of as-prepared CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator in two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) devices, the corresponding tests were employed. For the 2D solar evaporator (2D-CDs/CF/CS-LF) test: the samples were floated on the surface of a beaker filled with brine and supported by a 3 mm thick (1.8 mm center radius) EPE sponge, and for the 3D solar evaporator (3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF) test: the sample was positioned on a thermal insulation assembly (polyurethane foam wrapped by absorbent cotton cloth) and the evaporation device was placed in a dish (r = 5 cm). Water evaporation collection system: the evaporation unit is placed inside a transparent sealed condensation unit with pipes connecting the inlet and outlet and a water storage container. The whole system is placed outdoors to utilize sunlight for desalination. For the treatment of dye wastewater and acid and alkali wastewater, these wastewaters are used as a source of water and fresh water is collected in a water evaporation collection system.
In addition, corresponding material characterization, testing instrumentation and calculation of solar vapor energy efficiency are provided in the Supporting Information.

CDs/CF/CS-LF composite hydrogel with excellent water supply and photothermal conversion capability was synthesized based on CDs, CS, CF and LF components (Fig. 1a-c). Among them, as a natural biopolymer, CS was used as a raw material for the preparation of hydrogels [27], while, as biocompatible photothermal carbon-based nanomaterials, CDs were effectively dispersed and immobilized in the hydrogel [28–30]. For improved the mechanical strength and stability of the hydrogel, subject by the “binding effect” of plant cell wall, LF as a natural 3 D skeleton supported the hydrogel evaporator against its shape deformation, while CF, as the filler, was added to the composite system as packing, further enhancing the mechanical properties. The as-prepared dried hydrogels were employed for the next structurally characterization by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). In Fig. 1d1, the CS-LF hydrogel exhibited a smooth surface with thin pore walls and smaller overall spatial volume. Compared with the former, with the addition of filler CF, the CF/CS-LF hydrogel exhibited a crosslinked three-dimensional structure and more continuous structure (Fig. 1d2). While the abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs interacted with the hydroxyl groups on the CS chains through hydrogen bonding, which promoted the polymerization and cross-linking of the chitosan chains [23], forming a more stable and thicker pore wall, and effectively avoiding the collapse of the pores (Fig. 1d3 and d4). Eventually, the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel obtained under the synergistic effect with continuous crosslinked network-like porous structure could facilitate the effective transport of sufficient water from the overall reservoir to the evaporation surface, and also serve as a salt drainage channel to effectively diffuse salt into the entire reservoir. For the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis in Fig.S2, the characteristic peaks of carbon appeared at near aound 25° for CS-LF and CF/CS-LF with low crystallinity, while after the addition of CDs, carbon peaks with high crystallinity were observed for CDs/CF/CS-LF, which indicated that CDs were successfully doped into the hydrogel. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra in Fig.S3 revealed an obvious characteristic peak of CDs at 1720 cm−1 can be found, corresponding to the CO stretching vibrations [31–33], and also can be seen in CDs/CF/CS, demonstrating that the CDs with CO bonds (from the carboxyl and ketone groups) was successfully crosslinked to the CS chain. Furthermore, the broad peaks in the range of 3180–3600 cm−1 for all samples are the characteristic absorption bands of the -OH stretching vibration [22,34,35], indicating that the as-synthesized material possesses outstanding hydrophilic and contributes to the improvement of seawater evaporation efficiency. In the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig.S4), the specific surface areas of CS-LF, CF/CS-LF and CDs/CF/CS-LF samples were calculated according to Brunel Emmett Taylor (SBET) were 1.53 m2/g, 2.51 m2/g, and 1.64 m2/g, respectively, indicating that the three-dimensional pore structure was not blocked after the addition of CF and CDs, which can still maintain the function of mass water transfer during the solar steam production process [36].

Conventional CS hydrogel has mechanical property defects such as fragility and decomposition [37], which are unfavorable for the long-term stable operation of the evaporator, while the mechanical and elastic properties of CS hydrogel were greatly improved after the addition of CDs, CF and LF. In order to verify the mechanical property of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel, the mechanical properties of CS, LF and CS-LF were firstly tested in compression (Fig. 2a), in which the stress-strain curve of CS demonstrated nonlinear behavior within the strain range of 10–40 %, where irreversible plastic deformation occurred, leading to structural damage and inability to recover due to the poor resilience of CS (Fig. 2b). Upon the delicate combination of CS and LF, the CS-LF hydrogel exhibited excellent compression recovery owing to the homogeneous dispersion mechanism of the natural fiber continuous skeleton of LF, conferring excellent ductility and avoiding permanent deformation after compression. In compression tests (Fig. 2c), CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel was capable of springing back to its initial form after compression, showing excellent compressive elasticity, and the compression curves were further analyzed specifically for different strains, in which the stress-strain curves of CS-LF, CF/CS-LF and CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogels exhibited linear behavior at 30 % strain, with the hydrogels maintaining linear elastic properties and rapidly returning to their initial states without significant structural damage. During compression tests, the stress-strain curve of CS-LF hydrogel turned to be nonlinear after the strain exceeded 30 % (Fig. 2d), while the enhancement effect of CF led to a nonlinear change of the stress-strain curve of CF/CS-LF hydrogel until the strain exceeded 50 % (Fig. 2e), and what's more, the stress-strain curves of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel still maintained linear changes before the strain exceeded 60 % (Fig. 2f), which was attributed to the fact that the added CDs facilitates the polymerization and cross-linking of CS chains [22,39], significantly enhancing the load-bearing capacity of this hydrogel, so that CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel maintained linear elastic behavior even at high stress and withstood deformation over a wide range of stress without permanent damage. Then following the fatigue test with 50-cycle of compression at 50 % strain, the stress-strain curves and force-displacement curves of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel remained intact and smooth without significant fluctuations (Fig. 2g-h), which indicated that the structure formed in the hydrogel was robust enough to almost return to its original shape without losing its elasticity during the rigorous compression and release tests, meanwhile, the maximum stress was increased by nearly 120 % from 230 kPa to 510 kPa, and the modulus of elasticity was raised to 0.0746 kPa compared with CS-LF (Fig. 2i).

Heat transfer process from the solar energy driven evaporator input by solar energy, steam energy output, and energy loss environment, so in this case, the inefficiency of the 2D evaporator is due to a part of the energy of heat exchange being transferred to the environment (Fig. 3a and b) [38]. While changing the macrostructure of a 2D evaporator from conventional 2D structure to 3D structure could significantly increase the evaporation area, efficiently harvest the ambient energy, and decrease the heat transfer loss. But simply increasing the exposure area of the evaporator was of little assistance in improving the evaporation rate, which required to sufficient water supply must be ensured along with the increase in exposure area, otherwise an inefficient evaporation due to insufficient water transfer or severe salt crystallization at the evaporation interface would be exposed [40]. In this work, due to CDs/CF/CS-LF capillary-permeable dual water supply mode and the binding effect of the LF, which realized water supply and water storage, enabling stable and efficient evaporation with the 3D solar evaporator. The evaporation rates of solar evaporators (R = 3.5 cm, h = 1 cm) were tested with simulated sunlight (Fig. 3c) and the mass loss of water during evaporation under 1 sun irradiation was recorded (Fig. 3d), of which the evaporation rates of the 2D-CDs/CF/CS-LF (2.42 kg m−2 h−1) evaporator was superior to CF/CS-LF (1.2 kg m−2 h−1), CS-LF (0.6 kg m−2 h−1) and pure water (0.2 kg m−2 h−1), attributing to the addition of the CDs enhanced the photothermal conversion capacity of the evaporator surface [33,41,42]. Unfortunately, the downward heat loss could not be avoided in the conventional 2D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator, so the heat-insulating component was introduced to reduce the heat loss and improve the evaporation performance [43]. moreover, due to the water-absorbent cloth around the heat-insulating component with effective water-transporting ability, sufficient water supply was ensured for the evaporation process, so that the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator possessed a high evaporation rate of 8.08 kg m−2 h−1 and a high efficiency of 93.75 % (Fig. 3e). The top evaporation rate (4.92 kg m−2 h−1) and the blank evaporation rate (0.45 kg m−2 h−1) of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator were further measured, which was inferred to obtain the side evaporation rate (2.71 kg m−2 h−1) (Fig. 3f), confirming that the side of the evaporator could derive more energy from the environment [44]. Notably, the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator could reach an evaporation rate of 6.09 kg m−2 h−1 even at 0.5 light intensity (Fig. 3g), which is significant for practical application in the special case of insufficient solar irradiation. In addition, the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator still maintained high evaporation rate in the concentration of salt water from 0 to 25 wt% (Fig. 3h), and the evaporation rate could reach 7.46 kg m−2 h−1 even for 25 wt% high concentration saline. In Fig. 3i, the DSC analysis curves indicated the reduced enthalpy of water evaporation in the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF structure, which provided the evidence of the reduced energy barrier for water evaporation in the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF structure, and the enthalpy of vaporization of water during solar evaporation was further estimated (Fig. 3j) [45,46]. Benefiting from its excellent water transport, outstanding thermal confinement, and reduced energy demand for water evaporation, the evaporation rate of 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF exhibits higher than that of most reported biomass-based solar evaporators (Fig. 3k) [44,49–57].

One of the critical aspects of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel possessed a high evaporation efficiency is the effective absorption of sunlight, thus the optical absorption property of as-prepared samples was measured using a UV–visible-Near Infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrophotometer (Fig. 4a), and the results show that the CS-LF hydrogel exhibited a lower photo-absorption, while the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel raised up to 92 %, due to the effective immobilization of CDs as a photothermal material in the hydrogel network [22]. The photothermal conversion performance of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel under simulated light irradiation was further investigated by infrared (IR) thermal camera (Fig. 4b), in which the surface temperature of CS-LF and CF/CS-LF hydrogels increased relatively slowly and finally stabilized at 30 °C ~ 31 °C (Fig. 4c), while the surface temperature of the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel was greatly improved under light exposure, with the temperature rapidly rising to 52.73 °C within 5 min, attributed to the photothermal conversion ability conferred by the CDs [33,47,48]. The desirable evaporation performance of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel is related to its excellent thermal management capability, and the thermal conductivity of CS-LF, CF/CS-LF and CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogels was measured (Fig. 4d), among which the prepared samples all exhibited low thermal conductivity close to that of conventional insulating foams (0.04 W m−1 K−1), attributed to the natural insulating pores of hydrogel, while the LF also offered more pores and cavities [49]. To further confirm the significance of surface structure in photothermal interface evaporator, the surface roughness of the prepared hydrogels was examined by laser confocal scanning microscopy. The surface roughness of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel in Fig. 4e1 was significantly increased in compared to CF/CS-LF (Fig. 4e2) and CS-LF hydrogels (Fig.S5), which was partly due to the swelling of the hydrogel evaporator as a whole induced by CF resulting in more pores, and the incorporation of CDs formed irregular protrusions on the evaporator surface, increasing the effective area of the light absorption interface and further augmenting surface roughness. In order to explore why the 3D evaporator had such a high evaporation efficiency, the IR thermal camera was used to test the surface temperature change of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel in 2D and 3D devices under simulated evaporation experiments. In Fig. 4f1, the maximum temperature on the surface of 2D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator was 31.5 °C, which was about 7.7 °C higher than that of 2D-CS-LF evaporator, while with the introduction of a thermal insulation component in the evaporation system, the surface temperature of 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator rapidly increased to 38.5 °C within 20 min, reaching a maximum equilibrium temperature of 41.2 °C (Fig. 4f2). Such a difference between 2D and 3D evaporators was because the direct contact of 2D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator with water could generate a heat transfer loss, leading to an overall temperature drop (Fig. 4g1) [57–59]. In contrast 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator displayed greater heat localization (ΔT = 15.4 °C), confining most of the heat to the evaporation surface for reducing heat loss and promoting vapor production (Fig. 4g2). The thermal management capability of the evaporator was then further evaluated by simulating the temperature distribution of the as-prepared evaporation system through COMSOL multiphysics software, in which demonstrated that the 3D-CD/CF/CS-LF evaporator displayed greater heat localization capability (Fig. 4h1), in contrast to the 2D-CD/CF/CS-LF evaporator that transferred most of the heat to the bottom (Fig. 4h2), which were consistent with the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Property of water transfer, anti-salt deposition and structural stability
ce:section.ce:para: Important factors for the continuous operation of solar evaporators included strong water transport capability, anti-salt deposition and stable structure, which needs to be carefully considered [43]. As we know, hydrophilicity was one of the prerequisites for the excellent water transport capability of solar evaporators [36]. Based on the surface contact angle tests of as-prepared samples, the water droplet was found to disappear quickly upon contact with the upper surface of CS-LF hydrogel (Fig. 5a), which was attributed to the hydrophilicity of the CS hydrogel itself [49]. When the CDs with hydrophilic functional groups was immobilized in the hydrogel, the hydrophilicity was further enhanced. Then, methyl blue solution was applied to visually verify the water transport capacity of the evaporator (Fig. 5b), in which the surface of the CS-LF hydrogel was rapidly stained (20 s), while the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel (5 s) achieved a further rapid surface staining with the help of the CDs with hydrophilic functional groups, proving that the water transport performance was also further improved. The strong water transport capability could provide enough water for the solar evaporation interface, endowing the evaporator with excellent self-cleaning ability, which was further verified by observing the salt dissolution behavior on the surface of the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel. In Fig. 5c, 1.5 g of NaCl was dispersed on the upper surface of the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel, and the salt particles were completely dissolved after 50 min. In addition, no salt particles were observed to be deposited on the surface of the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel after continuous operation for 5 h at different salt concentrations (3.5 %, 7 %, 15 %, and 25 %) (Fig. 5d), which is attributed to its excellent water transfer ability and re-dissolution of salt particles, proving the outstanding anti-salt deposition ability of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel. Stable structure could ensure the continuity of the water supply and avoid the deposition of salt particles on the surface of the solar evaporator, so it was necessary to investigate whether the structure of the evaporator is damaged in the wet state or not. In Fig. 5e, the CDs/CF/CS hydrogel possessed an inhomogeneous asymmetric structure, while the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel structure was homogeneous and symmetric. After 60 min of irradiation, the upper region of the CDs/CF/CS hydrogel obviously contracted and underwent asymmetric shape deformation, compared to the overall shape of the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel that exhibited no significant change, which benefited from the “binding effect” of the LF skeleton. Meanwhile, water absorption capacity ensured that the evaporator possesses outstanding evaporation rate (Fig. 5f), in which the CS hydrogel water absorption ratio was 8.99 (water absorption: 4.75 g) due to the hydrophilicity of the hydrogel, and the water absorption of the CS-LF hydrogel (water absorption: 10.76 g) and the CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel (water absorption: 12.76 g) were further improved when the introduced LF skeleton endowed the evaporator with a stabilized structure. Generally speaking, the swelling change of the hydrogel could destroy the water transmission channel of the evaporator, resulting in reduced water supply, and then causing the deposition of salt particles on the surface of the evaporator [20]. For above reason, the shape changes of CDs/CF/CS and CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel was observed in dry and water-saturated condition (Fig. 5g), in which the volume of CS hydrogel changed significantly before and after swelling, on the contrary, the shape of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel remained unchanged. Then, in order to accurately understand the swelling pattern, the changes in the swelling ratio of the prepared samples in solutions with different conditions were further recorded (Fig. 5h). The results exhibited that the swelling ratio of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogel was stable and less than 18 % in high salt seawater and different pH, which was attributed to the “binding effect” of the LF skeleton, in contrast to the swelling behavior of the CDs/CF/CS hydrogel by the salt concentration and pH significantly. Cycle compression tests intuitively displayed the excellent water absorption capacity of CDs/CF/CS-LF hydrogels, which quickly absorbed spilled water within 3 s, even after 50 compression-release cycles (Fig. 5i).

As we know, in remote and infrastructure-deficient islands, the scarcity of freshwater resources not only impeded residents' daily drinking water needs but also significantly constrained local agricultural production, particularly crop which were sensitive to the salinity and alkalinity of water during their growth stages [60,61]. For this purpose, a small-scale solar desalination system comprising a solar evaporator, a condensation device, and associated storage tanks, was designed with the aim of utilizing the abundant sunlight to collect purified water for crop cultivation (Fig. 6a, b and Fig.S7). During the experiment, seawater from the Bohai Sea (coordinate (E120.548, N36.093), in China) was used as the actual seawater source, and the evaporation device was placed on the open rooftop of the experimental building, which solar radiation intensity and water collection quantity (kg m−2) were recorded on the experimental day (May 24, 2024, Zhenjiang, China). In the experiment conducted from 8:00 a.m. to 17:00 p.m. (Fig. 6c). under natural sunlight, the condenser rapidly became turbid due to water vapor condensation after only 5 min of sunlight irradiation, and a significant amount of condensate appeared on the condenser after 30 min. In Fig. 6d, the evaporation rate of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF varies with solar intensity, reaching a maximum evaporation rate of 5.4 kg m−2 h−1 at 12:00 (the time of peak solar intensity), with a solar radiation intensity of 0.94 kW m−2. Impressively, over the 9-h test period, a total of 34.88 kg m−2 of purified water was produced, demonstrating the outstanding outdoor evaporation performance of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator. The ion concentrations of the purified water collected from the outdoor experiments were analyzed using inductively coupled plasma (ICP) spectroscopy. Compared to natural seawater, the ion concentrations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+) in the purified water from the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator were significantly reduced by 3–4 orders of magnitude (Fig. 6e), meeting the World Health Organization (WHO) standards for drinking water, highlighting the excellent desalination performance [62]. Furthermore, assessing the water purification performance of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator under various environmental conditions (such as organic dyes, acidic, and alkaline solutions) was crucial. As in Fig. 6f and g, the freshwater collected from simulated organic dye (Methylene blue, MB and Rhodamine B, RhB), acidic (CH3COOH), and alkaline (NaOH) solutions exhibited excellent quality, with no detectable organic dyes (MB and RhB) and neutral pH levels, fully demonstrating the superior performance of 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator in removing organic dyes and handling acidic and alkaline solutions. The desalinated water collected eventually was used to germinate bean sprouts to explore the potential of desalinated seawater in practical applications, with seawater and tap water as comparisons. In Figs. 6h, i and S8, bean sprouts cultivated with natural seawater were unable to grow at all due to the high salt concentration. In contrast, bean sprouts cultivated with purified water grew well, similar to those cultivated with tap water, reaching approximately 18 cm in 9 days. The above results indicate the safety, cleanliness, and compatibility of the purified water for crop growth, proving the potential of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator in bean sprout cultivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seawater desalination mechanism of 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF hydrogel evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the aforementioned experimental results and in-depth analysis, the solar evaporator desalination mechanism of the conventional 3D solar evaporator and binding effect-supported 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF solar evaporator were compared and presented in Fig. 7 . In Fig. 7a, the conventional solar evaporator confronted with low evaporation rate, which mainly stemmed from its inefficient water transport due to its randomized water transport path [43,61]. During continuous evaporation, this randomness not only decelerated the water transfer rate, but also inevitably triggered salt crystallization on the evaporation surface as evaporation proceeded. Crucially, the presence of salt crystals severely hindered light absorption, reflected sunlight, blocked vapor evaporation channels, and reduced the effective evaporation area, thereby tremendously decreasing the photothermal conversion efficiency and evaporation rate. For comparison, the binding effect-supported 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF illustrated in Fig. 7b achieved ultra-high solar steam generation through a well-designed structure, which is mainly reflected in the following aspects: (i) the evaporator utilized LF with “binding effect” to support the CDs/CS/CF hydrogel to construct a directional water transport path, which combined capillarity and osmosis to dramatically accelerate the water transport rate and facilitate the return of salt ions from the evaporating surface to the main water body; (ii) by enhanced filling, CF significantly improves the mechanical properties of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator, ensuring better shape and performance under external pressure or impact and achieving stable water supply; (iii) CDs are effectively immobilized in the hydrogel network, increasing surface temperature of the 3D-CDs/CS/CF-LF evaporator, while the low thermal conductivity of CDs enhances the thermal localization effect, which facilitates the continuous and rapid vapor production on the evaporator surface.

A “binding effect”-supported 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator was systematically investigated, which possessed an ultra-high solar steam evaporation rate of up to 8.08 kg m−2 h−1 and an excellent photo-thermal conversion (93.75 %), significantly surpassing traditional CS-based evaporators. Herein, LF served as a natural 3D skeleton to support the hydrogel evaporator to resist shape deformation, and formed an efficient capillary-osmosis dual water supply mode with the CDs/CF/CS hydrogel, enabling the evaporator to maintain a high evaporation rate even in high-salinity brine, demonstrating exceptional anti-salt deposition capabilities. Moreover, the addition of CF as a nanofiller enhanced the mechanical property of the CS hydrogel, promoting structural stability and stable water supply during evaporation, while CDs further improved the mechanical property of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator through hydrogen bond-mediated aggregation and cross-linking of chitosan chains. Simultaneously, and meanwhile CDs as photothermal material, were effectively fixed within the hydrogel network, enhancing the photothermal conversion capability of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator. Further outdoor seawater desalination experiments validated the efficiency and stability of the 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator in practical applications, meeting the needs for potable water and agricultural irrigation. The 3D-CDs/CF/CS-LF evaporator proposed in this work featured significant advantages in mechanical property and evaporation performance, which provided a reference for further improving the evaporation rate of 3D-CS-based evaporators.
